perturbation.original_text: All questions concerning the construction, validity, enforcement and interpretation of this IP Agreement shall be determined in accordance with the provisions of the Investment Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: All questions concerning the construction, validity, enforcement and interpretation of this IP Agreement shall be determined at the Company's sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The original text defers to the Investment Agreement for interpreting the IP agreement. The modified text gives the company the sole right to interpret, which may contradict the requirement for 'good faith and fair dealing' implied in contracts under New York law, particularly where the Investment Agreement might offer more balanced interpretations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. By granting the Company sole discretion in interpreting the agreement, it allows the Company to potentially act in bad faith, undermining the investor's reasonable expectations and contradicting the implied covenant. This could lead to legal challenges as interpretations could be skewed to benefit the Company unfairly. This is especially relevant as the original text refers to another agreement, suggesting a shared understanding between both Parties for interpretations.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-12-26 2015-01-02 2015-02-20 2015-02-27 2016-05-20 2016-08-26 2016-09-16 2018-08-31 2018-10-05 2021-12-03 2022-01-28 2022-06-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 1-304 Obligation of Good Faith Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) CHAPTER 38, ARTICLE 1, PART 3 Section 1--304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.
perturbation.justification: The modified text grants the Company sole discretion in interpreting the IP Agreement, whereas the original text referred to the Investment Agreement. This change contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing required in every contract as per NY UCC § 1-304. By giving the company sole discretion, the modified text allows for potentially biased interpretations, undermining the investor's reasonable expectations, and thus breaching the obligation of good faith in the contract's performance and enforcement.

perturbation.original_text: Investor will notify Company of any suspected infringement of the Patents. Subject to Article 7.01 below, the sole right to institute a suit for infringement of the Patents rests with Company. Investor agrees to reasonably cooperate with Company in such suit for infringement, including requesting Investor's employees or consultants to testify when requested by Company in writing, making available records, papers, information, specimens, and the like, provided and only to the extent such is reasonably necessary to prosecute the suit. For the avoidance of doubt, nothing in this Article 7.00 shall require Investor to join any such suit as a party, and Company shall not seek to join Investor to any such suit as a party absent the express written consent of Investor.
perturbation.changed_text: Investor will notify Company of any suspected infringement of the Patents. Subject to Article 7.01 below, the sole right to institute a suit for infringement of the Patents rests with Company. Investor agrees to cooperate with Company in such suit for infringement. For the avoidance of doubt, nothing in this Article 7.00 shall require Investor to join any such suit as a party, and Company shall not seek to join Investor to any such suit as a party absent the express written consent of Investor.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of 'reasonably' before cooperate and the clause specifying forms of cooperation removes a level of protection for the investor. This obligates the investor to cooperate without defining reasonable limits, potentially requiring them to provide information that could be commercially sensitive, which may violate trade secret laws if the request is too broad. The removal of the words 'including requesting Investor's employees or consultants to testify when requested by Company in writing, making available records, papers, information, specimens, and the like, provided and only to the extent such is reasonably necessary to prosecute the suit' also removes investor's protections on what they must cooperate on.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text, by removing the 'reasonableness' clause and specified forms of cooperation, potentially forces the investor to disclose trade secrets during cooperation with the Company's infringement suit. This violates the Trade Secrets Act which protects against the misappropriation of trade secrets. The original text allowed some protection. By making the cooperation obligation unconditional and undefined, the contract creates a legal risk of forced disclosure.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the 'reasonableness' qualifier and specific examples of cooperation, potentially forcing the investor to disclose trade secrets, which contradicts the spirit of trade secret protection laws. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 18 USC 1836 regarding civil proceedings, implies protection for trade secrets against misappropriation. The original text provided some protection by limiting cooperation to what was 'reasonably necessary.' By removing this limitation, the modified contract creates a risk of forced disclosure, potentially violating trade secret laws by obligating the investor to cooperate without defined, reasonable limits.

perturbation.original_text: Company shall indemnify and hold harmless Investor, Investors' Affiliates, and their officers and directors, and their direct and indirect customers, to the fullest extent permitted by applicable law, from and against any and all losses, liabilities, obligations, claims, contingencies, damages, diminution in value, deficiencies, actions, proceedings, taxes, costs and expenses, including all judgments, amounts paid in settlements, court costs and reasonable attorneys' fees and costs of investigation as incurred, arising out of or relating to any breach of any of the representations made in this Article 3.09.
perturbation.changed_text: Company shall indemnify and hold harmless Investor, Investors' Affiliates, and their officers and directors, and their direct and indirect customers, from and against any losses, liabilities, obligations, claims, contingencies, damages, diminution in value, deficiencies, actions, proceedings, taxes, costs and expenses arising out of or relating to any breach of any of the representations made in this Article 3.09.
perturbation.explanation: The omission of 'to the fullest extent permitted by applicable law' limits the scope of indemnification, potentially contradicting state laws (like California, where Investor is located) that mandate certain minimum levels of indemnification for corporate officers and directors against liabilities arising from their roles. Additionally, the removal of 'including all judgments, amounts paid in settlements, court costs and reasonable attorneys' fees and costs of investigation as incurred,' limits the Investor's indemnification regarding legal fees which is a violation of indemnification.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Corporations Code Section 317 allows for and in some cases mandates the indemnification of corporate agents (including officers and directors) for expenses, judgments, fines, settlements, and other amounts reasonably incurred in connection with threatened, pending, or completed actions or proceedings. The modified text removes the phrase 'to the fullest extent permitted by applicable law' and the examples of coverage, which weakens the indemnification protection afforded to Investor's officers and directors, potentially making it non-compliant with California law if a situation arises where indemnification is legally required but not fully covered under the modified clause. Specifically, the omission of 'reasonable attorneys’ fees' contradicts typical indemnification coverage.

CORPORATIONS [100 - 14707] ( Title 1 enacted by Stats. 1947, Ch. 1038. ) DIVISION 1. GENERAL CORPORATION LAW [100 - 2319] ( Division 1 repealed and added by Stats. 1975, Ch. 682. ) CHAPTER 3. Directors and Management [300 - 318] ( Chapter 3 added by Stats. 1975, Ch. 682. ) 317. (a) For the purposes of this section, “agent” means any person who is or was a director, officer, employee or other agent of the corporation, or is or was serving at the request of the corporation as a director, officer, employee or agent of another foreign or domestic corporation, partnership, joint venture, trust or other enterprise, or was a director, officer, employee or agent of a foreign or domestic corporation which was a predecessor corporation of the corporation or of another enterprise at the request of the predecessor corporation; “proceeding” means any threatened, pending or completed action or proceeding, whether civil, criminal, administrative or investigative; and “expenses” includes without limitation attorneys’ fees and any expenses of establishing a right to indemnification under subdivision (d) or paragraph (4) of subdivision (e). (b) A corporation shall have power to indemnify any person who was or is a party or is threatened to be made a
perturbation.justification: The omission of "to the fullest extent permitted by applicable law" and "including all judgments, amounts paid in settlements, court costs and reasonable attorneys' fees and costs of investigation as incurred" significantly weakens the indemnification protection provided to the Investor's officers and directors. This contradicts California Corporations Code Section 317, as evidenced by Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states that "expenses" includes "attorneys' fees and any expenses of establishing a right to indemnification". The changed text's removal of these specific items limits the scope of indemnification, creating a clear contradiction with the state law's mandate for indemnification of expenses, including attorneys' fees.
